title: Reserve live-status badges for literal live states
summary: Flag decorative live-status badges when the surrounding interface does not represent a real-time, active, or currently broadcasting state.
instruction: Use live-status badges only for a literal active process, real-time feed, production state, or broadcast that can be verified from the interface context.
rationale: A live badge communicates operational truth. Using it as generic decoration creates false urgency, weakens trust, and makes genuine system states harder to distinguish from marketing ornament.

violations: A static SaaS hero displays a green LIVE badge above an ordinary product screenshot.
A pricing card labels the annual plan LIVE without any active process or event.
controls: A streaming page marks the currently broadcasting channel Live.
A deployment dashboard marks the production service Live after a successful health check.
borderline: A dashboard labels a data feed Live but does not show when the feed last updated.

exceptions.description: Literal operational, broadcast, event, and real-time data states may use a live badge when the state can be verified from nearby context.

detector.signalDescription: Identify live-status labels and evaluate whether nearby copy, state metadata, timestamps, or interface controls establish a literal live process.
limitations: Source text alone may not prove whether a backend state is genuinely live.
Quoted user content and event titles must not be treated as interface status labels.

scenarios: Operational dashboards, streaming products, sports applications, and market-data tools use live literally.
mitigations: Require badge-like presentation plus missing evidence of a real-time state before reporting the semantic concern.

fixturePlan.description: Static marketing hero with decorative green live badge and no real-time behavior.
Streaming interface with a verifiable current broadcast and live timestamp.
Data-feed dashboard using a live badge without a last-updated indicator.

notes: This proposal is intentionally semantic and should not become a universal word blacklist.
